original_hypothesis.title: Division by Zero Blocks End-of-Vesting Transfers to New Beneficiaries
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: In _createVesting, when a new beneficiary receives a transfer and numOfSteps == _stepsClaimed (all steps elapsed), releaseRate = amount / (numOfSteps - _stepsClaimed) causes division by zero. This prevents transferring vesting tokens to new addresses after the full vesting period, blocking both marketplace purchases and direct transfers at end-of-vesting.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Marketplace purchases at end-of-vesting (when all steps have elapsed but tokens remain unclaimed) will revert, locking tokens in VestingManager. The buyer pays but the completePurchase call fails.

proof_of_signal.pattern: releaseRate: _totalAmount / (numOfSteps - _stepsClaimed) — when _stepsClaimed == numOfSteps, divides by zero

preconditions: All vesting steps have elapsed (block.timestamp >= endTime)
Transfer target is a new address (no existing vesting record)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: _totalAmount / (numOfSteps - _stepsClaimed) where both equal numOfSteps → division by zero
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Any marketplace purchase after full vesting period reverts for new buyers. Listed tokens become permanently locked in VestingManager.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Not profit-driven — funds locked permanently. Sellers cannot recover listed tokens if unlistVesting also triggers this path.

economic_questions: How many listings could be active near end-of-vesting?
Does unlistVesting also hit this path (transferring back to seller at end of vesting)?
Can existing beneficiaries (with existing vesting records) still receive transfers?
validation_requirements: Set block.timestamp > endTime, attempt transfer to new address
Test with existing beneficiary (should take else branch, no div-by-zero)

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: All active listings on expired vesting plans become unpurchasable

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Sellers with listings on expired plans, buyers attempting late purchases
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All listed tokens on vesting plans near/past expiry

validation_result.mechanism: division by zero in new-beneficiary release rate calculation
validation_result.impact_summary: When a grantor with stepsClaimed == numOfSteps transfers tokens to a new beneficiary, _createVesting divides by (numOfSteps - _stepsClaimed) = 0, reverting. This blocks transfers to new addresses and marketplace listings under specific end-of-vesting conditions.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The _createVesting function has two branches: one for new beneficiaries (totalAmount == 0) and one for existing ones. The existing-beneficiary branch (else) correctly guards against division by zero with an explicit check: `if (numOfSteps - _vestings[_beneficiary].stepsClaimed != 0)`. However, the new-beneficiary branch (if) has NO such guard, computing `releaseRate: _totalAmount / (numOfSteps - _stepsClaimed)` unconditionally. When `_stepsClaimed == numOfSteps`, this divides by zero and reverts.

The concrete trigger scenario:
1. User A has a vesting with numOfSteps=10. After all steps elapse, A calls claim() — stepsClaimed becomes 10, amountClaimed = totalAmount.
2. Token issuer calls createVesting(A, newAmount) adding more tokens. Since A's totalAmount > 0, the ELSE branch runs. The guard `numOfSteps - stepsClaimed == 0` fires, setting releaseRate = 0. A now has: stepsClaimed=10, totalAmount=oldTotal+newAmount, amountClaimed=oldTotal, unclaimed balance = newAmount.
3. Token issuer (or vestingDeployer) calls transferVesting(A, B, someAmount) where B is a brand-new address with totalAmount=0.
4. Balance check passes (unclaimed = newAmount >= someAmount).
5. _createVesting(B, someAmount, 10, true) executes. B's totalAmount == 0, so the NEW beneficiary path runs: releaseRate = someAmount / (10 - 10) → DIVISION BY ZERO. Transaction reverts.

The same issue affects marketplace flows. If VestingManager's totalAmount reaches 0 (all tokens sold/returned), and a seller with stepsClaimed == numOfSteps attempts to list, _createVesting for VestingManager as new beneficiary triggers the same division by zero during listing.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The defect is clearly visible by comparing the two branches of _createVesting. The else-branch (existing beneficiary) has explicit protection at approximately lines 197-203, while the if-branch (new beneficiary) at approximately line 191 lacks this protection. The inconsistency proves the developers were aware of the edge case but missed applying the guard to the new-beneficiary path.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The new-beneficiary branch (if) computes releaseRate = _totalAmount / (numOfSteps - _stepsClaimed) without checking for zero divisor. The existing-beneficiary branch (else) handles this exact case with a conditional check. When _stepsClaimed equals numOfSteps, the new-beneficiary path reverts with division by zero.

transferVesting passes grantorVesting.stepsClaimed to _createVesting. If grantor has fully claimed (stepsClaimed == numOfSteps) but has unclaimed balance from later-added tokens, the division by zero is triggered for new beneficiaries.

calling_sequence: 1. Token issuer creates vesting for user A (stepsClaimed=0, totalAmount=1000, numOfSteps=10)
2. All steps elapse, A calls claim() → stepsClaimed=10, amountClaimed=1000
3. Token issuer calls createVesting(A, 500) → ELSE branch runs (A.totalAmount > 0), releaseRate=0, totalAmount=1500, amountClaimed=1000
4. A now has 500 unclaimed tokens with stepsClaimed=10
5. Token issuer calls transferVesting(A, B, 200) where B is a new address
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: transferVesting → _createVesting with _stepsClaimed == numOfSteps. The new-beneficiary branch computes releaseRate: _totalAmount / (numOfSteps - _stepsClaimed) without a zero-divisor guard. The existing-beneficiary branch has this guard (lines ~197-203), proving the developers intended to handle this case but missed it in the if-branch.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment configuration affects this vulnerability. It is a pure code logic defect in _createVesting.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The scenario requires: (1) a grantor whose stepsClaimed equals numOfSteps, and (2) the grantor having unclaimed balance (from tokens added via createVesting after full claim), and (3) a transfer to a new address (totalAmount == 0). This is achievable when token issuers top up vestings after the vesting period, a realistic administrative action. For the marketplace path, the division by zero occurs during listing (when VestingManager has totalAmount=0 and the listing seller has stepsClaimed=numOfSteps), preventing the seller from listing entirely.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH severity. Users who fully claim their vesting and then receive additional tokens (via createVesting) cannot transfer those tokens to new addresses. This blocks marketplace listings for affected sellers (if VestingManager's totalAmount is 0 at listing time) and blocks direct off-market transfers to new beneficiaries. Tokens remain claimable by the user directly but cannot be redistributed. The inconsistency between the two branches in _createVesting is a clear code defect.
recommended_mitigations: Add a zero-divisor guard to the new-beneficiary branch in _createVesting, mirroring the existing guard in the else-branch: if (numOfSteps == _stepsClaimed) { set releaseRate to 0 } else { compute normally }
Consider setting releaseRate = _totalAmount when stepsClaimed == numOfSteps and this is a new beneficiary, allowing immediate claim of the full amount via the catch-all in claimable()
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy StepVesting with numOfSteps=10. 2. createVesting(userA, 1000). 3. Warp time past endTime. 4. userA calls claim() → stepsClaimed=10, amountClaimed=1000. 5. tokenIssuer calls createVesting(userA, 500) → succeeds (else-branch). 6. tokenIssuer calls transferVesting(userA, newUserB, 200) → EXPECT REVERT (division by zero).
uncertainties: The marketplace purchase path requires VestingManager's totalAmount to be exactly 0 (all previous tokens sold/returned) AND the listing seller to have stepsClaimed == numOfSteps. This compound condition may be rare in practice.
The direct transferVesting path via tokenIssuer/vestingDeployer is the most straightforward trigger but requires admin-level access to call createVesting followed by transferVesting.
additional_testing_needed: Verify the exact revert reason (Solidity 0.8 panic code for division by zero vs custom error)
Test marketplace listing flow when VestingManager's totalAmount is 0 and seller has stepsClaimed == numOfSteps
Test unlistVesting returning tokens to a seller whose totalAmount became 0 (seller transferred everything away)
